title: spawnOne() silently swallows factory exceptions, hiding pool starvation cause
description: In the `acquire()` method's spawn-under-cap loop, a bare `catch { break; }` at line 508 swallows any exception thrown by `this.factory()` or `spawnOne()`. The caller receives fewer handles than requested with no diagnostic about why the factory failed (OOM, Worker construction error, etc.). The empty catch makes it impossible to distinguish 'pool at cap' from 'factory threw', and the pool may silently degrade to serial mode without logging.
evidence: Verified at lines 498-509: bare `catch { break; }` with no logging or re-throw. A factory that throws (e.g. Worker constructor OOM) silently causes the pool to return fewer handles than requested, with the caller unable to tell whether the cap was hit or the factory failed.

title: Worker addEventListener wiring failures silently swallowed, hiding lifecycle errors
description: In `spawnOne()`, `worker.addEventListener` calls are wrapped in try/catch with a `wiringOk = false` fallback 'for test doubles'. If real Worker construction succeeds but addEventListener throws (e.g. a subclassed Worker with a bug), the pool marks `wiringOk = false` and calls `worker.terminate()` but the thrown error is completely lost. The only observable effect is `throw new Error('lifecycle wiring failed')`, which loses the original cause.
evidence: Verified at lines 619-625: the catch blocks swallow the original exception and only set `wiringOk = false`. The downstream throw at wiring failure loses the root cause. However this is low-likelihood in production (real workers don't throw on addEventListener), so the practical impact is moderate.

title: ensureLoaded() silently swallows all postMessage errors per worker
description: In `ensureLoaded()`, the entire postMessage dispatch for a worker is wrapped in a bare `catch {}` (line ~758). A postMessage failure (detached SAB, worker already terminated, structured-clone error on large bytes) is silently ignored. The worker will later receive a decode request without having the bytes loaded, and it will reply with UNKNOWN_BYTES_ID. The actual cause (postMessage throw) is never surfaced.
evidence: Verified at line 758: bare `catch {}` with no logging. If postMessage throws (e.g. SAB not transferable, worker terminated), `set.add(bytesId)` is not reached so the worker won't be considered loaded, but the decode request proceeds anyway leading to a silent UNKNOWN_BYTES_ID failure later.

title: Watchdog timeout uses incorrect 1.5x multiplier when requestTimeoutMs is zero or undefined
description: When `requestTimeoutMs` is undefined (the common case where no requestTimeout is set on the pool), the watchdog timeout is computed as `Math.max(10_000, (requestTimeoutMs ?? 0) * 1.5)` = `Math.max(10_000, 0)` = 10 seconds. This is correct by coincidence, but the intent is unclear. More critically, when `requestTimeoutMs` is 0 (explicitly disabled), the watchdog still fires at 10 seconds and kills the worker, which may be intentional but is not documented. The guard `if (!job.requestTimer)` on line 155 is correct, but the fallback arithmetic reads as if the watchdog is derived from requestTimeout when it is not.
evidence: The severity label is 'high' but the finder self-acknowledged 'The current code is correct by coincidence'. The watchdog always fires at >= 10s whether or not requestTimeoutMs is set; this is a documentation issue not a bug. No concrete failure scenario exists — the behavior is intentional and the 10s floor is the actual design.

title: destroy() setInterval leak when active workers never drain: interval not cleared on grace timeout
description: In `destroy()`, a `setInterval` polling loop is created to wait for `this.active.size === 0`. If the active workers never drain within `graceMs`, `Promise.race` resolves via the grace timeout, but the `setInterval` `iv` is never `clearInterval`'d because the interval's own `r()` callback was never called. The interval continues firing every 10 ms for the lifetime of the environment until `active.size` eventually hits 0 (which may never happen if workers were force-terminated). This is a timer leak.
evidence: Verified at lines 397-402: `iv` is captured inside the Promise constructor closure and only cleared when `active.size === 0` fires first. If the grace timeout wins the race, `iv` is unreachable and never cleared. After `destroy()` returns, the force-terminate loop at lines 405-407 zeros `active`, so the leaked interval does eventually fire and self-clear — but this relies on the post-await code path running, which is not guaranteed if the caller discards the destroy() promise. The leak window is real.

title: getOrParseJxtcTileIndex: tilesX * tilesY multiplication can overflow before bounds check
description: In `getOrParseJxtcTileIndex`, `numTiles = header.tilesX * header.tilesY` is computed in plain JS number arithmetic. If both are near `2^16`, their product overflows JS integer precision (exceeds 2^32). The size check `bytes.byteLength < 32 + numTiles * 8` can produce a correct guard in IEEE double but `new Uint32Array(numTiles)` with ~4e9 elements would throw RangeError.
code_excerpt:
const numTiles = header.tilesX * header.tilesY;
  if (bytes.byteLength < 32 + numTiles * 8) { ... }
evidence: Verified at line 92: no cap on `numTiles` before the Uint32Array allocation. `parseJxtcHeader` caps imageW/imageH at 2^24 but `tilesX = Math.ceil(imageW/tileSize)` with tileSize=1 could yield tilesX near 2^24; product of two such values overflows to ~4e9 and `new Uint32Array(~4e9)` throws RangeError, crashing the parse rather than giving a clean PyramidError. A pre-cap check like `if (numTiles > 1e7) throw ...` is missing.

title: ensureIccProfile silently swallows all errors and always resolves null on failure
description: The inner async IIFE in `ensureIccProfile` has a blanket `catch { return null; }` that discards all decode errors. An OOM, WASM crash, or JXL parse error on a valid file will silently produce `iccProfile = null` with no diagnostic. Since this result is then cached on the source object, subsequent calls also return null.
evidence: Verified at lines 413-415: blanket `catch { return null; }` with no logging. The cached Promise is stored at line 417 so all subsequent calls return the same null. Colour-managed display paths silently fall back to sRGB without any indication that ICC extraction failed.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled acquires handles but never waits for ready before fallback decision
description: At line 1257, `await Promise.all(liveHandles.map((h) => h.ready))` waits for all handles to be ready, but throws if any handle's `ready` promise rejects. The `ready` promise is only resolved (never rejected per code), so this is safe. However the fallback path when `usable.length === 0` skips `buffersInFlight` cleanup.
evidence: The `ready` promise in spawnOne is only ever resolved (via `_readyResolve`), never rejected — the `onDeath` path calls `destroyHandle` which calls `_readyResolve()` (not reject). So `Promise.all(h.ready)` cannot throw. The `usable.length === 0` path correctly calls `p.release(liveHandles)` at line 1260. The `buffersInFlight` leak is already captured as concurrency-6. This finding as stated is not independently valid.

title: Progressive Phase 1 DC errors with 'fail-fast' policy propagate as non-PyramidError, losing typed error code
description: In the dc-then-final Phase 1 loop, when `errorPolicy !== 'skip-tile'`, the caught `e` from `decodeTileBytesProgressive` is re-thrown bare (`throw e`). If the error is a plain `Error` or DOMException, callers cannot distinguish tile parse failure from infrastructure failure.
evidence: Verified at lines 377/386: `throw e` re-throws without wrapping. The outer catch in decode-level.ts at line 125-128 does wrap non-PyramidErrors into `BAD_REGION`, so the code IS eventually wrapped — but loses the specific `JXTC_PARSE` code. The pooled path wraps uniformly into PyramidError; the direct path does not, creating inconsistent error taxonomy for fail-fast tile errors.

title: prewarmAsync fire-and-forget in constructor creates unhandled promise rejection on spawn failure
description: In the `PyramidWorkerPool` constructor, `void this.prewarmAsync(this.minIdle)` discards the returned promise. If `prewarmAsync` throws (e.g. `this.factory()` throws synchronously in `spawnOne`), the rejection is unhandled.
evidence: Verified at line 310: `void this.prewarmAsync(this.minIdle)` with no `.catch()`. The `prewarmAsync` body has no top-level try/catch — only the `h.ready.catch(()=>{})` guard at line 358 for post-spawn ready failures. A synchronous throw inside the `for` loop at line 352 (from `spawnOne`) propagates out of `prewarmAsync` as an unhandled rejection. Also confirmed at line 801 in `getOrCreatePool`: `void p.prewarmAsync(2)` is similarly unguarded.

title: Viewport cache completeness check silently treats skipTiles.size > 0 as incomplete, but may cache partial result anyway via cacheStore
description: The `complete` guard at line 416 correctly excludes viewports with skipped tiles from being cached. This is correct behavior, not a bug.
evidence: The finder's concern is that a caller who previously decoded with skipTiles then decodes without skipTiles won't find a cache hit. But this is the correct design — the first partial decode was intentionally not cached as complete. The second full decode (no skipTiles) will run normally and populate the cache. No bug exists.

title: decodeTilesParallel: error from one coroutine aborts remaining but does not remove outer abort listener before throwing
description: If `Promise.all` itself throws, `outerAbortListener` cleanup happens in `finally`. The risk is duplicate abort calls on already-aborted controller.
evidence: The `finally` block always runs, so `outerAbortListener` IS removed regardless of whether `Promise.all` throws. Calling `controller.abort()` on an already-aborted AbortController is a no-op per spec. No observable failure scenario exists.

title: Aspect ratio validation uses division without guarding height=0
description: At line 160, `width / height` with `height=0` produces `Infinity`. The aspect check then throws a confusing message about aspect ratio inconsistency rather than zero height.
code_excerpt: if (Math.abs(aspect - width / height) > 1e-3) { ... }
evidence: Verified at line 160: no zero-height guard before the division. `requireNumber` only checks `isFinite`, so `height=0` passes. `width/0 = Infinity`, and `Math.abs(aspect - Infinity)` is always `Infinity > 1e-3`, so validation always fails for zero height with a misleading aspect-ratio message instead of a clear 'height must be positive' error.

title: release() destroys handles with pending jobs but does not reject their promises before destroy
description: In `release()`, when a handle has `h.pending.size > 0` it calls `destroyHandle(h, 'release of dead/inflight')`. `destroyHandle` calls `cleanupPendingJob` + `job.reject(POOL_DESTROYED)` for each pending job.
evidence: Verified: `destroyHandle` at lines 427-443 calls `cleanupPendingJob` which calls `job.reject`. The `doReject` helper checks for already-settled promises via the pending map lookup. The scenario described (abort path manually deletes from pending before reject) is protected by the `h.pending.has(id)` guard in `doReject`. No actual double-reject scenario is reachable.

category: no structured error taxonomy / sentinel errors
title: PyramidErrorCode uses open `string & {}` union, preventing exhaustive handling by callers
description: The `PyramidErrorCode` type includes `(string & {})` as a fallback preventing exhaustive switch/case checking.
code_excerpt: export type PyramidErrorCode = ... | (string & {});
evidence: Verified at lines 205-222: `(string & {})` is the last member. This is a real design trade-off that prevents TypeScript exhaustive checking. The opportunity is genuine — a separate `UnknownPyramidErrorCode` type would allow callers to write exhaustive switch statements for the known codes.

title: acquire() maxWaitMs defaults to 60ms with no diagnostic when timeout fires
description: The `acquire()` method uses a 60ms default maxWaitMs. When timeout fires, waiter is removed and `resolve(got)` is called with partial handles with no logging.
code_excerpt: const maxWait = opts?.maxWaitMs ?? 60;
evidence: Verified at lines 461-537: the timeout fires and calls `resolve(got)` silently. No logging, no indication that fewer handles were returned due to timeout vs cap. Adding a structured result or console.warn would make pool-sizing issues diagnosable in production.

title: decodeTileBytesProgressive: events() async iterator abandonment on decoder.push() throw leaks decoder
description: In `decodeTileBytesProgressive`, if `decoder.push()` throws, the `finally` block disposes the decoder but `drainOutcome` is not awaited before exit.
evidence: Verified at lines 496-504: if `decoder.push()` throws, control jumps to `finally` which disposes the decoder, but `drainOutcome` is never awaited. The `drainOutcome` IIFE iterates `decoder.events()` which may continue trying to read from a disposed decoder. The abandoned Promise can produce an unhandled rejection if the events() iterator throws after dispose.

title: getOrCreatePool: pool.destroy(0) called fire-and-forget when factory changes, hiding destroy errors
description: When factory conflict is detected with no active decodes, `void pool.destroy(0)` is called fire-and-forget. `pool = null` and `poolFactory = null` are set synchronously before destroy completes.
evidence: Verified at lines 785-787: `pool = null` is set synchronously before the async `destroy(0)` resolves. The old pool's timers continue running against an orphaned state. However the `destroy()` body force-terminates all workers, so inflight timers will at most fire on the now-orphaned pool object — the main risk is the timer leak from errors-5 combining with this pattern.

title: deadlineHit failedTiles loop uses hardcoded level=0 instead of source.level
description: When budget deadline is hit, lines 393-401 build failedTiles using `tileIdOf(t, source.tileSize, 0)` (hardcoded level 0). Phase 1 and 2 use `source.level ?? 0`. For pyramid level > 0, TileId.level mismatches cause all tiles to spuriously appear in failedTiles.
code_excerpt: const id = tileIdOf(t, source.tileSize, 0);  // BUG: should be source.level ?? 0
evidence: Verified at line 395: hardcoded `0` while line 242 in Phase 1 correctly uses `source.level ?? 0`. The `stitchedFinal` set contains keys built with the correct level, so for any source.level > 0, the deadline-hit tile IDs won't match any stitchedFinal entry, causing every tile to be added to failedTiles even when most decoded successfully. Also independently confirmed as contracts-014.

title: dc-then-final pooled path fires final onTile for cache-hits before the final decode pass runs
description: In decodeTiledViewportPooled's dc-then-final branch, 'final' stage onTile events for hit tiles fire before the actual final decodeTilesParallel pass runs. The `progressBase` is then set including these pre-fired finals.
evidence: Verified at lines 1325-1329: `onTile` is called with `stage: 'final'` for cache-hit tiles before `decodeTilesParallel` runs for miss tiles at line 1343. Consumers receive 'final' paint notifications for hits before any miss tiles have started decoding, making progress reporting order non-monotonic and the completed/total counts misleading during the decode.

title: convergedByteEnd == bytes is accepted but should be rejected as non-truncating
description: The validator only fails when `cbe > bytes`, allowing cbe == bytes through. But `pickByteEndForQuality` uses strict less-than (`pt.bytes < level.bytes`), so a convergedByteEnd equal to bytes is silently ignored at runtime.
code_excerpt: if (cbe > bytes) fail(`${path}.convergedByteEnd`, `${cbe} exceeds bytes ${bytes}`);
evidence: Verified at line 116: `cbe > bytes` allows equal values through. This creates silent no-op manifest entries — a stored `convergedByteEnd` equal to `bytes` is useless and misleading to manifest authors. The fix is `cbe >= bytes`.

title: Cache quality key for dc-only mode: pooled path always uses 'final' cache key
description: The pooled path in tiled-decode-pool.ts:1119 always uses `'final'` for the cache quality key, ignoring the dc-only progressive option. The direct path at decode-level.ts:160 correctly computes `cacheQuality = progressive === 'dc-only' ? 'dc' : 'final'`.
code_excerpt:
// tiled-decode-pool.ts line 1119:
    const cacheKeyFinal = cache ? viewportCacheKey(getLevelId(source.bytes), vp, plan.format as PixelFormat, 'final') : undefined;
evidence: Verified at tiled-decode-pool.ts:1119: hardcoded `'final'` for `cacheQuality`. However dc-only progressive is excluded from the pooled path entirely by the `parallelEligible` guard at decode-level.ts:139 (`progressive === 'dc-then-final' || progressive === undefined`). So dc-only never reaches the pooled path — the bug is dormant. Keeping as confirmed but with reduced real-world impact since the guard prevents the divergence from being reached.

title: JXTC safety cap uses 1<<30 integer shift which is 0 in JS for large values due to 32-bit sign
description: Line 64 checks `totalBytes > (1 << 30)`. `1 << 30` is correct (1073741824). The concern is if ever changed to `1 << 31` it would break. Current code is correct.
code_excerpt: if (!Number.isFinite(totalBytes) || totalBytes > (1 << 30) || imageW > (1 << 24) || imageH > (1 << 24)) {
evidence: The finder acknowledged 'The current code is correct'. `1 << 30 = 1073741824` is positive and correct in JS. This is a latent documentation concern about a hypothetical future edit, not an actual bug. No concrete failure exists.

title: decodeTilesParallel total progress count is halved for dc-only progressive mode
description: Line 926: `total = opts.progressTotal ?? (tiles.length * (opts.progressiveStage ? 2 : 1))`. For dc-only mode with progressiveStage='dc', this gives tiles.length * 2, but dc-only only does one pass.
code_excerpt: const total = opts.progressTotal ?? (tiles.length * (opts.progressiveStage ? 2 : 1));
evidence: The pooled dc-only path is excluded by the `parallelEligible` guard in decode-level.ts:139 (dc-only never reaches decodeTiledViewportPooled). It's unclear whether `decodeTilesParallel` is ever called directly with progressiveStage='dc' from a dc-only context outside the excluded path. The bug is real in the function's contract but may be unreachable in practice.

title: Handle state transition table allows WarmFloor -> WarmFloor self-transition to pass in DEV mode
description: The `cur !== next` guard means any self-transition silently passes without logging, masking potential double-activation bugs.
code_excerpt: if (DEV && !allowed[cur]?.includes(next) && cur !== next) {
evidence: Self-transitions being no-ops is standard idempotency design for state machines. The guard `cur !== next` is intentional to avoid throwing on harmless redundant calls. No actual bug exists — the concern is speculative about hypothetical double-activation bugs that would require other code to be wrong first.

title: armAllExcessIdle iterates in LIFO (hottest) order but reaping should prefer FIFO (coldest)
description: armAllExcessIdle iterates from `idle.length - 1` down to `minIdle`, arming timers on the hottest handles first. Coldest handles (idle[0]) are left without timers.
evidence: Verified at lines 590-593: iterates from `idle.length-1` (hottest, LIFO push) down to `minIdle`. The hottest workers get timer-armed first, meaning they're most likely to be reaped. The coldest workers (at index 0) are below `minIdle` and never armed. This is backwards for cache efficiency — coldest should be reaped preferentially. The LIFO pop in `acquire` means index 0 is least likely to be used next.

title: decodeWhole viewport cache uses getLevelId(bytes) as ID but bytes is a Uint8Array, while tiled path uses getLevelId(source)
description: decodeWhole at line 563 builds the cache key as `getLevelId(bytes)` where `bytes` is the raw Uint8Array. The tiled path uses `getLevelId(source)` (LevelSource WeakMap). For the same underlying buffer they produce the same ID (getLevelId delegates to bytesId for Uint8Array and uses bytesId of source.bytes for LevelSource), so they should match.
code_excerpt: const key = `${getLevelId(bytes)}-${nomW}x${nomH}-${format}-whole`;
evidence: Verified at cache.ts:59-68: `getLevelId(source)` for a LevelSource calls `bytesId(source.bytes)` (line 65) which is the same as `getLevelId(source.bytes)` = `bytesId(source.bytes)`. Both paths produce the same ID for the same underlying buffer. The finder's concern about divergent IDs is incorrect — the WeakMap key for LevelSource stores `bytesId(source.bytes)` as the value.

title: Opportunity: validateLevel does not verify that w, h, bytes are positive integers
description: requireNumber only checks that values are finite; it does not reject w=0, h=-1, or bytes=0.
evidence: Verified at lines 85-87: `requireNumber` only checks `isFinite`. Zero or negative w/h pass validation. The aspect ratio check at line 160 would catch zero height (produces confusing Infinity error) but not negative values. The opportunity is genuine — explicit positivity checks would give clearer error messages earlier.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled outBuffer cache-hit path checks cached.length <= ob.byteLength but never checks cached.length >= need
description: The outBuffer cache-hit path guards with `cached.length >= need && cached.length <= ob.byteLength`. If cached.length > ob.byteLength, the hit falls through to a full redecode.
evidence: Verified at lines 1126-1129: the condition `cached.length >= need && cached.length <= ob.byteLength` is actually correct. `ob.byteLength >= need` is guaranteed by the check at line 1125. If cached.length > ob.byteLength, we can't copy it into ob, so falling through to redecode is the right behavior. The condition is not broken — it correctly handles the case where a cached tile is larger than the output buffer.

title: makeTiledSource helper omits tilesX/tilesY from the returned LevelSource object
description: The test's `makeTiledSource` helper creates JXTC header bytes with tilesX=2, tilesY=1 but the returned object literal does not include `tilesX` or `tilesY` fields.
evidence: Cannot verify without reading the test file. The finding identifies a test helper defect — missing `tilesX`/`tilesY` on LevelSource. If these fields are required by LevelSource type, TypeScript would flag this at compile time. The actual runtime impact depends on whether decode paths read `source.tilesX` directly or derive them from the JXTC header bytes.

title: complete condition uses && skipTiles.size > 0 but should use || to detect any incompleteness
description: The finder self-corrected: 'No bug here'. The semantics are correct — complete iff no deadline, no fails, no skips.
code_excerpt: const complete = !deadlineHit && failedTiles.length === 0 && !(skipTiles && skipTiles.size > 0);
evidence: The finder explicitly concluded 'No bug here' within the finding description itself. The condition is logically correct: all three conditions must hold simultaneously for a viewport to be considered complete. This is a confirmed false positive by the finder's own analysis.

title: Opportunity: tilesOverlappingRegion property test does not verify full coverage
description: The random property test verifies individual tile bounds but not that the union of all tiles covers the entire region (no gaps).
code_excerpt:
if (w > 0 && h > 0) expect(covered).toBeGreaterThan(0);
    // Missing: full coverage = region.w * region.h
evidence: The opportunity is genuine — `covered > 0` only checks that at least one tile exists, not full coverage. A tiling bug that returns only a subset of tiles would pass this test. Adding `expect(covered).toBe(region.w * region.h)` for non-edge cases would provide a meaningful regression guard.

title: Unvalidated opaque `metadata` object passed through verbatim from untrusted manifest JSON
description: At line 196, `result.metadata = o['metadata'] as Record<string, unknown>` stores the raw parsed JSON value with no field-count limit, key-length cap, or depth cap.
code_excerpt: if (o["metadata"] !== undefined) { requireObject(o["metadata"], "manifest.metadata"); result.metadata = o["metadata"] as Record<string, unknown>; }
evidence: Verified at line 196: only `requireObject` (non-array check) is applied; no depth cap, key count limit, or key sanitization. A hostile manifest with deeply nested metadata or very many keys can cause quadratic iteration if callers spread/iterate it. The risk is real but medium severity since V8 JSON.parse prevents `__proto__` injection.

title: ProducedBy.params stored without sanitisation allows attacker-controlled opaque dict
description: `validateProducedBy` assigns `params` verbatim to the result after only a `requireObject` check.
evidence: Same pattern as security-1. Verified at lines 73-76: only type check, no depth/size sanitization. The params object is opaque and could contain deeply nested structures or very large key counts.

title: Level numeric fields (w, h, bytes, tileSize, cols, rows) have no upper-bound checks, enabling OOM via crafted manifest
description: The validator verifies finite numbers but places no cap on magnitude. A hostile manifest with e.g. `w = 2^40` passes validation.
evidence: Verified at lines 85-87: `requireNumber` only checks `isFinite`. A manifest with `w=2^40` passes validation. `createLevelSource` applies a 1-GiB cap for the whole-level branch but the tiled branch relies on `parseJxtcHeader` which caps at `1<<30` — the manifest w/h can be larger than the JXTC header and create confusing dimension mismatches or OOM in pixel allocation paths.

title: contenthash accepted as any non-empty string with no format constraint, enabling cache-key confusion
description: The `contenthash` field is validated only for non-emptiness. An attacker can inject a contenthash containing colons (used as delimiters in `makeLevelCacheKey`).
code_excerpt:
const contenthash = requireString(o["contenthash"], `${path}.contenthash`);
  if (contenthash.length === 0) fail(`${path}.contenthash`, "must not be empty");
evidence: Verified at lines 92-93: only length > 0 check. The `makeLevelCacheKey` function (not read directly, but described) prepends `ch:` without escaping — a contenthash like `foo:bar:final` could collide with tile cache keys. The risk is real for cache key confusion attacks.

title: master.name accepted as arbitrary string with no path-traversal sanitisation
description: The `name` field in `MasterMetadata` is only required to be a string with no path character restrictions.
code_excerpt: const name = requireString(o["name"], `${path}.name`);
evidence: Verified at line 57: `requireString` only checks typeof. No length cap, no path-character rejection. The name is used for display/logging, but if any ingest tooling constructs file paths from it (likely given this is a file management system), path traversal is possible. Low severity since it's currently display-only, but the latent risk is real.

title: GalleryIndex.next pagination cursor accepted without format validation
description: The `next` field is validated only as a non-undefined string. If used directly as a URL, a hostile manifest could redirect to arbitrary origins.
code_excerpt: if (o["next"] !== undefined) result.next = requireString(o["next"], "index.next");
evidence: Verified at line 218: no URL format validation. The severity depends entirely on whether callers use this value as a URL without further validation. Without reading the consumers of `GalleryIndex.next`, this cannot be confirmed as an exploitable SSRF vector.

title: ensureIccProfile writes result onto an arbitrary source object via dynamic property assignment
description: `ensureIccProfile` caches its result by writing `(source as any)['_iccProfile'] = p` directly onto the caller-supplied object.
code_excerpt:
(source as any)[key] = p;
  return p;
evidence: Verified at line 417: `(source as any)[key] = p` stamps a Promise onto an arbitrary object. If source objects are serialized (JSON.stringify for telemetry), the Promise appears as `{}`. Using a WeakMap keyed on source would be cleaner and avoid polluting the object's enumerable properties.

title: parseWorkerReply trusts worker-supplied w/h dimensions without upper-bound checks
description: In `parseWorkerReply`, a `decode-reply` with `ok: true` is accepted if `typeof d.w === 'number'` with no cap on w/h.
evidence: Verified at lines 829-834: only typeof checks on w/h. The subsequent `minBytes = reply.w * reply.h * bpp` at line 660 with very large w/h would produce Infinity, and `pixels.byteLength < Infinity` is always true, bypassing the size guard. A compromised worker could return w=2^30, h=2^30 causing the stitch path to use an undersized pixel buffer.

title: Hand-rolled validator duplicates Zod/Valibot pattern without exhaustive numeric-range contracts
description: The entire manifest-validate.ts is hand-rolled. It omits numeric range contracts and string format contracts.
code_excerpt:
// manifest-validate.ts
// Hand-rolled runtime validation for PyramidManifest and GalleryIndex.
evidence: Verified: manifest-validate.ts is entirely hand-rolled with ~235 lines. Multiple security findings (security-1 through security-5) document missing range/format checks that a schema library would enforce systematically. The opportunity to add numeric range contracts is well-evidenced.

title: No evidence of npm audit or Dependabot configuration for transitive dependency vulnerabilities
description: No visible `npm audit` step in CI, no Dependabot/Renovate config.
code_excerpt: import { createDecoder, decodeTileContainerRegionRgba8, decodeTileContainerRegionRgba16 } from "@casabio/jxl-wasm";
evidence: Cannot confirm absence of CI config without reading .github/workflows or package.json scripts. The finding may be accurate but requires infrastructure files outside the reviewed code paths to verify.

title: Module-level singleton pool has no mutex: concurrent callers can race to create/swap the pool
description: The module-level `pool` and `poolFactory` variables in `getOrCreatePool` are read and mutated without any lock. Two concurrent calls with the same factory can both see `pool === null` and both assign, leaking the discarded pool.
code_excerpt:
let pool: PyramidWorkerPool | null = null;
let poolFactory: (() => WorkerLike) | null = null;
evidence: Verified at lines 776-804: `getOrCreatePool` is a synchronous function reading and writing module-level `pool`/`poolFactory`. Since JS is single-threaded, two concurrent *async* callers that both await before calling `getOrCreatePool` would see consistent state — but two callers that call `decodeTiledViewportPooled` concurrently before any `await` would both enter `getOrCreatePool` synchronously and both could see `pool === null` if the first call hasn't set it yet. This is a real TOCTOU in the synchronous execution window.

title: prewarmAsync state transition races: concurrent calls can push state back to Prewarming after Active
description: `prewarmAsync` unconditionally sets `this.state = PoolState.Prewarming` at line 348. Concurrent calls can interleave state transitions.
code_excerpt: this.state = PoolState.Prewarming;  // overwrites previous state unconditionally
evidence: Verified at lines 346-360: if `prewarm()` (fire-and-forget, line 364) is called while a prior `prewarmAsync` is in flight at line 358 (`await Promise.all(...)`), the second call re-sets state to Prewarming, then both settle to Active. While benign for correctness (JS single-threaded, no actual data race), the `acquire()` guard at line 456 checks for Active/Prewarming/Created — the state flip from Active back to Prewarming could surprise callers that check `pool.poolState` between awaits.

title: buffersInFlight check-then-add is not atomic: two concurrent decodes with the same outBuffer can both pass the guard
description: The code checks `buffersInFlight.has(outBuf)` and then calls `buffersInFlight.add(outBuf)` with several throw-able checks in between. If two async decode calls share the same outBuf, both can complete the `has` check before either does the `add`.
evidence: Verified at lines 151-156: `has` check at line 151, `add` at line 156, with throw-able alignment check at lines 152-155 between them. In JS single-threaded execution, both calls must complete the synchronous checks before either can `await` — but since both calls are synchronous up to this point, both COULD run the `has` check before either runs `add` if called in the same microtask tick. The fix is to `add` immediately after the `has` check.

title: destroy() leaks active-drain interval on exception and uses polling instead of a Promise-based drain
description: `destroy()` installs a `setInterval` to wait for active drain. If `Promise.race` itself throws, `clearInterval` is never reached.
evidence: Same underlying issue as errors-5. Verified: the `iv` reference is captured inside the Promise constructor closure. `Promise.race` does not throw in normal execution (both branches resolve), but after `destroy()` completes (line 409 sets Destroyed), the force-terminate loop at lines 405-407 zeros `active.size`, causing the leaked interval to fire and self-clear. The leak window is real but self-healing in practice.

title: decodeTilesParallel coroutine loop checks failed/aborted after awaiting, not before claiming tile index
description: Each coroutine reads `const idx = next++` before checking `failed || controller.signal.aborted`. One extra tile per coroutine is claimed after failure/abort.
code_excerpt:
while (true) {
      if (failed || controller.signal.aborted) break;  // abort check AFTER prior await
      ...
      const idx = next++;   // tile index consumed before confirming non-aborted
evidence: Verified at lines 946-952: the abort check is at the top of the loop but `next++` happens after — so on the iteration following an abort, one extra index is consumed per coroutine. For N coroutines, N tile slots are wasted after failure. `completedCount` undershoots total by N, and progress telemetry shows incorrect completion ratios.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled does not release acquired handles on the empty-handles early-exit path; buffersInFlight also not cleaned
description: After `p.acquire(desired)` succeeds, if `liveHandles.length === 0`, the code falls through to a direct decode and returns without the `finally` blocks that call `p.release` and `buffersInFlight.delete`.
evidence: Verified at lines 1252-1255: the early return at line 1254 is outside the inner `try` (line 1266) and also outside the outer `try` (line 1155). The `buffersInFlight.delete` at line 1383 is in the outer `finally` and is skipped. If `options.outBuffer` was provided, it is permanently stuck in `buffersInFlight` after this path, causing all future calls with that buffer to throw `BUFFER_IN_USE`.

title: ensureIccProfile assigns a Promise to source._iccProfile without guarding concurrent first-call callers
description: `ensureIccProfile` checks `if (key in source)` then assigns a Promise. The check and assign are synchronous so the window is zero in practice.
evidence: Verified at lines 392-417: the `in` check and assignment are synchronous with no `await` between them. In JS single-threaded execution, no second call can interleave between these two lines. The finder correctly noted 'the window is zero in practice'. Not a real concurrency issue.

title: Module-level idCounter and WeakMaps in cache.ts are not safe if multiple realm instances share the same module
description: Module-level `idCounter` and WeakMaps are realm-local. If accidentally imported in a worker, a second idCounter starts at 0 and collides with main-thread IDs.
code_excerpt: let idCounter = 0;  // module-level; realm-specific but undocumented
evidence: Verified at lines 36-51: module-level mutable idCounter with no realm check. The existing doc comment warns 'Cache IDs are realm-local' but this is passive documentation. A runtime assertion like `if (typeof WorkerGlobalScope !== 'undefined') throw new Error(...)` would make violations fail fast. The opportunity for a defensive check is genuine.

title: decodeTilesParallel pre-check for already-aborted signal only runs at registration, not at await point
description: If `opts.signal` is aborted between the `aborted` pre-check and the `outerAbortListener` registration, the bridge listener is registered but the internal controller is never aborted for that narrow window.
evidence: In JS single-threaded execution, `opts.signal` cannot change state between lines 916 and 918 — there is no `await` point between them. The window described by the finder is zero microseconds. Not a real concurrency issue.

title: Waiter queue in acquire() uses a plain setTimeout for expiry but does not cancel the timeout on early resolution
description: When a waiter is resolved early by `release()`, the setTimeout still fires (harmlessly calling resolve on an already-settled promise). Minor timer leak.
evidence: Verified at lines 515-529: waiter object has no timer reference. When `release()` resolves a waiter early via `w.resolve(give)` at line 574, the setTimeout still fires, calls `resolve(got)` on an already-settled Promise (silently ignored), and removes itself from `waiters` (already removed). One ghost timer per over-cap acquire. Confirmed minor but real.

category: missing abort propagation on pooled path
title: Fallback direct-decode paths inside decodeTiledViewportPooled do not check signal before starting decode
description: The fallback paths use `raceWithAbort` which correctly handles already-aborted signals. The outer `finally` buffersInFlight issue is already captured in concurrency-6.
evidence: The finder acknowledged 'If concurrency-6 is fixed, signal propagation is already correct.' The `raceWithAbort` at line 1253 correctly handles abort signals. This finding is subsumed by concurrency-6 and has no independent validity.

title: Per-tile grid-origin and expectedLen recomputed twice in the same function for cache hits and partial-hit paths
description: In decodeTiledViewportPooled, grid tile origin and expectedLen are computed in the cache-scan loop and again in the stitch paths.
evidence: Verified at lines 1280-1287: same arithmetic (col, row, gridTileX, gridTileY, gridTileW, gridTileH) recomputed in the cache-hit stitch loop for hits. Pre-computing these once alongside the hit/miss categorization would eliminate the duplicate. For large tile grids this is measurable overhead on the pan hot path.

title: Per-tile grid-origin arithmetic duplicated in dc-phase and final-phase of progressive dc-then-final direct path
description: Both Phase 1 and Phase 2 `runWithBoundedConcurrency` loops independently recompute tx, ty, srcOriginX, srcOriginY, decodedW, decodedH, and expectedLen for every tile.
code_excerpt: // identical block in phase 1 (~line 252) and phase 2 (~line 341)
evidence: Verified at lines 251-258 (Phase 1) and lines 341-348 (Phase 2): identical arithmetic blocks computing the same values from the same inputs. `tilesWithBytes` already pairs tiles with their bytes; adding a `tileGeom` field would eliminate the duplication across both phases.

title: O(n) deduplication of failedTiles using Set<string> + tileKey() allocates per-tile strings at end of decode
description: After decode phases, failedTiles are deduplicated by building a string-keyed Set via `tileKey(id)` on every entry.
evidence: Verified at lines 407-412: dedup loop allocates one string per failedTile entry. For the common case (0 failures) this is a zero-cost `if` check. For skip-tile paths with many failures, the string allocation is measurable. The improvement of using a pre-accumulated Set<string> during phase accumulation is real.

title: Two intermediate arrays (tileBytesList + tilesWithBytes) allocated for every progressive viewport decode
description: The progressive dc-then-final path allocates `tileBytesList` via `plan.tiles.map()` and then immediately allocates `tilesWithBytes` via a second `plan.tiles.map()`.
code_excerpt:
const tileBytesList: Uint8Array[] = plan.tiles.map((t) => extractTileBitstream(source.bytes, t, exHeader));
const tilesWithBytes = plan.tiles.map((t, idx) => ({ tile: t, bytes: tileBytesList[idx]!, idx }));
evidence: Verified at lines 209-215: two sequential `.map()` calls on the same `plan.tiles` array. These could be merged into one pass. For large viewports (256 tiles) this saves 256 intermediate array element allocations.

title: Center-out sort allocates a new array and recomputes distances per tile on every viewport decode
description: `tilesWithBytes.slice().sort()` allocates a new array and the comparator recomputes squared distances inline for each comparison without caching.
evidence: Verified at lines 220-224: standard Schwartzian transform gap. Pre-computing distances once into an array before sort would reduce O(n log n) distance computations to O(n). The `.slice()` allocation is also avoidable if sorted in-place on the already-allocated tilesWithBytes.

title: Center-out sort of misses array allocates and recomputes per viewport decode in pooled path
description: decodeTiledViewportPooled sorts the misses array with `misses.slice().sort()` and inline squared-distance comparator on every viewport pan event.
evidence: Verified at lines 1301-1305: same pattern as perf-5. Same fix applies: pre-compute distances once O(n), then sort with O(1) comparator.

title: _reapBound uses Array.includes() (O(n)) to check idle membership on every timer fire
description: The idle-reap timer callback calls `this.idle.includes(h)`, which is O(n) on the idle array.
code_excerpt: if (this.idle.includes(h) && this.idle.length > this.minIdle) {
evidence: Verified at line 687: `this.idle.includes(h)` is O(n). For typical pool size (maxSize=8) this is 8 comparisons max — negligible. But the pattern is avoidable with a `inIdle: boolean` flag on WorkerHandle or a Set<WorkerHandle> for idle membership, while keeping the LIFO array for pop semantics.

title: destroyHandle uses Array.indexOf() + Array.splice() to remove handle from idle list (O(n) scan)
description: destroyHandle scans the idle array with `this.idle.indexOf(h)` on every handle teardown. On `reapAllIdle()`, this is O(n^2).
code_excerpt:
const ii = this.idle.indexOf(h);
if (ii >= 0) this.idle.splice(ii, 1);
evidence: Verified at lines 439-440: `indexOf` + `splice` is O(n) per call. `reapAllIdle` iterates all idle handles calling `destroyHandle` on each, making the total O(n^2). For n=8 this is 64 operations — acceptable but avoidable.

title: setHandleState allocates a new allowed[] array on every state transition via object literal
description: `setHandleState` constructs a fresh `Record<HandleState, HandleState[]>` object literal every call, including 5 array literals.
evidence: Verified at lines 57-63: the `allowed` object and its 5 arrays are constructed fresh on every `setHandleState` call. In production (`DEV=false`), the `if (DEV && ...)` guard short-circuits but the object is still constructed before the check. Hoisting as a module-level const would eliminate all per-call allocations.

title: destroy() iterates pending jobs via Array.from(h.pending.values()) allocating a snapshot array
description: Both `destroy()` and failure paths snapshot `h.pending.values()` via `Array.from()`, allocating a temporary array.
code_excerpt: for (const job of Array.from(h.pending.values())) { ... }
evidence: Verified at line 385: `Array.from(h.pending.values())` snapshot. This is correct to prevent mutation-during-iteration but the allocation is avoidable — directly iterating `h.pending.values()` is safe here since `job.reject` removes from pending via `cleanupPendingJob`, which modifies the Map but doesn't invalidate the values iterator in JS (Map iterators are live but deletion during forward iteration is safe).

title: cacheStore always calls pixels.slice() even when byteLength == need (no surplus)
description: `cacheStore` calls `pixels.slice()` unconditionally, even when `byteLength == need` (full copy when trimming is not needed).
code_excerpt: cache.set(key, pixels.byteLength > need ? pixels.slice(0, need) : pixels.slice());
evidence: Verified at line 482: `.slice()` is always called (ownership copy). For the `byteLength > need` branch, `slice(0, need)` trims correctly. For the equal case, `.slice()` copies the entire buffer unnecessarily. A zero-copy opt-in flag or passing the buffer directly when already owned would avoid this allocation on the common equal-size path.

title: Per-tile final-cache and dc-cache lookups in Phase 1 cause N independent cache.get() calls with N string key allocations
description: For each tile in Phase 1, two template-literal strings are constructed and two `cache.get()` calls are made. With N tiles this is 2N string allocations and 2N map lookups. Phase 2 repeats, yielding 3N total key constructions.
evidence: Verified at lines 261-270 (Phase 1) and lines 351-352 (Phase 2): per-tile string allocations and cache lookups. `makeTileCacheKey` is called each time. Pre-building a Map of tile keys before the phase loops would reduce to N constructions total.

title: bytesId constructs a template-literal string on every call even on WeakMap hit path
description: `bytesId()` always allocates a `:${b}:${view.byteOffset}:${view.byteLength}` string even when the buffer is already in the WeakMap.
code_excerpt: return `${b}:${view.byteOffset}:${view.byteLength}`;
evidence: Verified at lines 45-52: the composite string is always constructed at line 51, even on WeakMap hit path (line 46-49 only caches the `B${n}` prefix, not the full composite). A second WeakMap keyed on the Uint8Array view identity (not just buffer) would cache the full composite string and make repeat calls O(1) with zero allocation.

title: No tile-decode throughput benchmark or regression gate exists for the jxl-pyramid package
description: The pan-60fps test has a very loose wall-clock guard (< 30s). No benchmark or CI gate measures tiles/second.
code_excerpt: expect(dur).toBeLessThan(30_000); // loose machine-indep guard
evidence: The 30-second guard is too loose to catch the per-tile alloc regressions identified in perf-1 through perf-13. A benchmark asserting decode throughput > N tiles/second would provide a meaningful regression gate, especially given the active optimization work on this module.

title: decodeTilesParallel coro fan-out creates one async closure per handle per viewport decode
description: `handles.map(async (h) => { while (true) {...} })` creates heap-allocated async closures per viewport decode.
evidence: Verified at lines 944-1028: one async closure per handle per `decodeTilesParallel` call. For dc-then-final with 2 handles, this allocates 4 closures per viewport decode. The fix (a shared coroutine function with handle as parameter) is complex. The finding is real but low-impact for typical pool sizes.

title: DecodedLevel.format is optional but all decode paths set it — callers cannot rely on its presence without a type guard
description: `format?: PixelFormat` is declared optional but every decode path populates it.
evidence: Verified at decode-core.ts line 7-19: `format?: PixelFormat`. Verified at decode-level.ts:404, 446, tiled-decode-pool.ts:1372: all paths set `format`. Making it required would allow callers to use it without null checks and catch regressions where a new code path forgets to set it.

title: ensureLoaded sends a SAB load message with non-standard fields (sab, byteLength) not defined in WorkerRequest type
description: The WorkerRequest type defines 'load' as `{ bytes: Uint8Array }` but `ensureLoaded` sends `{ sab, byteLength }` with `as any` cast when useSAB is true.
code_excerpt: h.worker.postMessage({ v: 1, type: 'load', bytesId, sab, byteLength: bytes.byteLength } as any);
evidence: Verified at line 753: `as any` cast bypasses type checking for the SAB variant. The protocol type doesn't declare the SAB shape. A worker implemented from the types alone would miss the SAB path, and the `as any` means TypeScript won't catch typos in the SAB variant fields.

title: validateLevel does not validate that w, h, bytes are positive integers — zero or negative values pass validation
description: Same as logic-10: `requireNumber` only checks `isFinite`. Zero/negative values pass.
code_excerpt: const w = requireNumber(o["w"], `${path}.w`);
evidence: Duplicate of logic-10. Verified: no positivity check on w, h, bytes at lines 85-87. Both finders independently identified the same gap.

title: parsePyramidManifest normalizes schema 1 to 2 but return type declares schema: 1|2
description: The function always writes `schema: 2` but return type is `PyramidManifest` with `schema: 1 | 2`, making callers think `manifest.schema === 1` is possible.
evidence: Verified at line 182: always `schema: 2`. The return type `PyramidManifest` (which includes `schema: 1 | 2`) is misleading. Any `if (manifest.schema === 1)` check after parsing will be dead code. The type should narrow to `schema: 2` or the normalization should be documented to prevent dead-branch confusion.

title: decodeTilesParallel resolves worker jobs with DecodedLevel missing format field
description: Worker job resolution at line 667 provides `{ pixels, width, height }` without `format`, violating the interface.
code_excerpt:
job.resolve({ pixels, width: reply.w, height: reply.h });
// format field missing
evidence: Verified at line 667: `job.resolve({ pixels, width: reply.w, height: reply.h })` — no `format`. The outer result at line 1372 sets format correctly, but tile-level DecodedLevel objects inside the parallel decode lack it. This is a latent type contract violation that would manifest if any consumer of per-tile results reads `.format`.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled uses getLevelId(source.bytes) for cache key but decodeTiledViewport uses getLevelId(source) — same source produces different cache keys across paths
description: The two paths use different getLevelId argument types for the same source, potentially producing different cache keys.
evidence: Verified at cache.ts:59-68: `getLevelId(source)` for a LevelSource object checks `levelIdBySource` WeakMap first, then if not found uses `bytesId(source.bytes)` as the value AND stores it. `getLevelId(source.bytes)` calls `bytesId(source.bytes)` directly. Both ultimately return `bytesId(source.bytes)` for the same source — they produce identical IDs. The finder's concern about divergence is incorrect.

title: parseJxtcHeader throws plain Error, not PyramidError — callers cannot catch by code and error taxonomy is broken
description: `parseJxtcHeader`, `getOrParseJxtcTileIndex`, and `extractTileBitstream` throw plain `new Error(...)` instead of `PyramidError` with code `'JXTC_PARSE'`.
code_excerpt: if (bytes.byteLength < 32) throw new Error("JXTC container too small for header");
evidence: Verified at tiling.ts lines 55-66: all throws use `new Error(...)`. The outer catch at decode-level.ts:125-128 wraps non-PyramidErrors into `BAD_REGION`, losing the `JXTC_PARSE` code. The `PyramidErrorCode` union already includes `'JXTC_PARSE'` specifically for these cases. Callers that catch `e instanceof PyramidError && e.code === 'JXTC_PARSE'` will never see this code for tiling.ts errors.

title: skipTiles completeness check: empty Set is correctly treated as no skips
description: When `skipTiles` is provided as an empty `ReadonlySet<string>` (size 0), the completeness check correctly evaluates to true — no tiles were skipped.
code_excerpt: const complete = !deadlineHit && failedTiles.length === 0 && !(skipTiles && skipTiles.size > 0);
evidence: The finder's concern is that if all tiles were in skipTiles, the viewport is cached despite being empty. But skipTiles is a Set of tile keys to SKIP (already decoded), not a set of failed tiles. A full-skipTiles decode (all tiles skipped) is a no-op by design — the target buffer already has the pixels from prior decode. Caching it as complete is correct.

title: Direct non-pooled path stamps iccProfile via `(result as any).iccProfile = icc` instead of typed field assignment
description: The `as any` cast bypasses TypeScript for setting the `iccProfile` field that is defined on the interface.
code_excerpt: if (icc) (result as any).iccProfile = icc;
evidence: Verified at lines 458 and 631: `(result as any).iccProfile = icc` in two places while line 1376 uses the typed `result.iccProfile = icc`. The `as any` casts are inconsistent and will silently break if `DecodedLevel.iccProfile` is renamed. The typed assignment form should be used throughout.

title: decodeTiledViewportPooled overloads omit 'pool' option from the Uint8Array first-argument form
description: The public overload at lines 1052-1063 accepts `{ parallel, decodeRegion, workerFactory, signal, useSAB }` but not `pool`. The implementation overload at line 1080 accepts `pool`. Callers using the Uint8Array form cannot pass `pool` in a type-safe way.
evidence: Verified at lines 1052-1075: the Uint8Array-first overload does not include `pool` in options. The `pool` option is only on the implementation signature at line 1080. A caller who passes `{ pool }` to the Uint8Array overload gets a TypeScript error even though the implementation handles it.

title: No runtime validation of outbound WorkerRequest messages
description: The pool sends WorkerRequest objects with no `parseWorkerRequest` mirror of `parseWorkerReply`. Breaking changes to request construction would produce silent timeout failures.
evidence: The inbound `parseWorkerReply` provides validation symmetry. The outbound path has no equivalent. A dev-mode assertion or `validateWorkerRequest` function would catch protocol drift early — especially given the `as any` cast on the SAB load message (contracts-002) which already bypasses type checking.

title: WorkerRequest/WorkerReply are versioned with literal v:1 but there is no version negotiation
description: All protocol messages carry `v: 1` but `parseWorkerReply` rejects any `d.v !== 1` silently. No handshake or capability advertisement.
code_excerpt: if (d.v !== 1) return null;
evidence: Version negotiation is genuinely absent. The `v:1` field was added with forward-compat intent but without a mechanism to detect/handle version skew. This is an acceptable architectural debt for now but the gap is real and would cause silent failures during any future versioned protocol upgrade.

title: Tiling descriptor cols/rows are not validated against w/h/tileSize
description: When `tiled=true`, `cols` and `rows` are only checked for finiteness, not cross-validated against `Math.ceil(w/tileSize)` and `Math.ceil(h/tileSize)`.
evidence: Verified at lines 107-111: no cross-validation of cols/rows against w/h/tileSize. A corrupt manifest with mismatched tile counts passes validation. The JXTC header's own tilesX/tilesY is used at parse time, so the manifest discrepancy only surfaces as a silent count mismatch, not a clear validation error.

title: deadlineHit path adds ALL non-stitchedFinal tiles to failedTiles using level=0, not source.level
description: Same bug as logic-1: `tileIdOf(t, source.tileSize, 0)` hardcoded instead of `source.level ?? 0`.
code_excerpt: const id = tileIdOf(t, source.tileSize, 0);  // should be source.level ?? 0
evidence: Duplicate of logic-1. Verified at line 395: hardcoded `0`. Both detectors independently caught this bug. The fix is replacing `0` with `source.level ?? 0` at line 395.
